planting.note: Plant after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May. Kong Mosaic coleus is a tender perennial that can be kept as a houseplant over winter in cooler climates or maintained as a perennial in zone 9 where light freezes kill back the stems but the roots often resprout. Pinch flower spikes promptly as they appear — coleus flowers are insignificant and pinching redirects energy to larger, more colorful foliage. In zone 9 the long warm season allows Kong Mosaic to develop into a large, impressive specimen.

directions: Plant in partial shade to shade in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil or container mix; Kong Mosaic is part of the Ball Horticultural Kong series of coleus — the largest-leaved coleus commercially available — with individual leaves reaching 6–12 inches across in a spectacular mosaic of burgundy, rose-red, chartreuse, and cream in irregular, patchwork-like patterns that vary across each leaf. The scale of the Kong series foliage creates a tropical, dramatic effect unlike any other shade foliage plant — plants placed in shaded beds or large containers make an immediate bold statement that smaller-leaved coleus and impatiens cannot replicate. Coleus has become significantly more versatile since modern selections like the Kong series were developed for improved sun tolerance — Kong Mosaic can be grown in partial shade to nearly full sun in cooler climates, though shade conditions produce the richest, deepest color. In zone 9 partial shade to shade is preferred to prevent leaf scorch. The vivid, multicolored foliage performs throughout the warm season without needing flowers to maintain the ornamental display.
sources: Ball Horticultural Company — Kong Series Coleus Introduction
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Coleus as Annual Foliage Plant
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Shade-Tolerant Annuals

planting.note: Plant after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May. Dragon Wing begonias are significantly more heat-tolerant than wax begonias and continue blooming through summer heat that causes other shade begonias to decline — an important advantage in zone 9 summers. In zone 9 the plant often overwinters with frost protection, developing into a large, impressive specimen in its second and subsequent years. Large containers (12–15 gallon) allow the plant to reach its full impressive size.

directions: Plant in partial shade to dappled light in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil or container mix; Dragon Wing Red is a large-growing cane-type (angel wing) begonia with pendant clusters of vivid crimson-red flowers hanging in graceful arching sprays above glossy, wing-shaped, asymmetric dark green leaves — the pendant flower clusters and arching canes create a weeping, tropical appearance unlike the mounded, dense form of tuberous or wax begonias. The large plant size (24–36 inches) and continuous, profuse flowering without deadheading make Dragon Wing Red the most impressive of the summer-blooming shade begonias for large container and landscape use. Unlike tuberous begonias and most wax begonias, Dragon Wing demonstrates significantly better tolerance for summer heat and maintains blooming performance through warm humid conditions — a critical advantage for zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens. The showy pendant flower clusters attract hummingbirds and are among the most visually dramatic of any shade-tolerant flowering plant.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Dragon Wing Begonia for Florida Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Heat-Tolerant Shade Annuals
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Container Annuals for Texas

planting.note: Plant after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May and again August–September. Impatiens walleriana is susceptible to impatiens downy mildew (IDM), which has devastated impatiens plantings across North America since 2011 — if IDM has been a problem in your area, consider New Guinea impatiens or SunPatiens (Impatiens hawkeri types) instead, which are resistant to IDM. The Accent series has demonstrated good performance when planted in well-drained conditions with good air circulation. Do not overhead water.

directions: Plant in partial shade to shade in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Accent Red Impatiens is part of the Accent series of Impatiens walleriana — the standard, traditional shade-flowering annual that has been the top-selling bedding plant in North America for decades due to its unmatched ability to produce continuous, vivid color in deep shade conditions where virtually no other flowering annual will bloom adequately. The compact, mounded habit, brilliant scarlet-red flowers, and vigorous production without deadheading made the Accent series the industry benchmark for impatiens performance. Impatiens walleriana (busy Lizzie) is the defining shade bedding plant for beds under trees, north-facing foundations, and deeply shaded borders — its natural woodland origin (East African highlands) gives it genuine shade tolerance rather than the partial-shade preference of most 'shade plants'. The continuous, prolific bloom and brilliant color saturation in true shade conditions remains a standard that few plants can match.
sources: All-America Selections — Impatiens Accent Series Awards
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Impatiens for Florida Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Annual Flowers for Shade

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring while dormant; in zone 9 plant December–February. Kieffer pear is partially self-fertile but bears more heavily with a cross-pollinator such as Orient, Moonglow, or another pear variety planted within 50 feet. In zone 9 Kieffer requires only 150–200 chill hours (temperatures below 45°F) to break dormancy — one of the lowest-chill requirements of any commercial pear, making it specifically well-suited to Gulf Coast and warm-winter gardens.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, deep, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Kieffer is an 1863 American pear hybrid (Pyrus communis x Pyrus pyrifolia) with extraordinary disease resistance and low chill hour requirements that make it the most reliably productive pear for hot-summer, mild-winter climates in the Southeast and Gulf South — regions where most European pear varieties fail from fire blight disease or insufficient winter chilling. The yellow-green to russet fruits with white, gritty, aromatic flesh are at their best when harvested slightly firm and allowed to ripen at room temperature for several days — the fresh eating quality improves dramatically with proper ripening off the tree. Kieffer pear is primarily a cooking and canning pear in American tradition: the firm, gritty flesh holds its shape beautifully in cooked preparations, and canned Kieffer pear preserves, spiced pears, and pear butter have been produced from this variety for over 150 years in Southern home kitchens. Exceptionally vigorous and productive once established, bearing full crops even in zone 9 conditions that exclude most pear varieties.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Pear Production for Gulf South
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Pear Variety Selection for Texas
University of Georgia Extension — Kieffer Pear for Home Orchards

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter while dormant; in zone 9 plant December–February. Fuji requires 300–500 chill hours (hours below 45°F) to break dormancy and fruit reliably — borderline for zone 9a (Houston receives approximately 300–400 chill hours annually). Plant in the most elevated, coldest microclimate available. Fuji requires a cross-pollinator (Gala, Granny Smith, or any other non-Fuji apple) planted within 50 feet for adequate fruit set.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, deep, fertile, slightly acidic soil in the best-drained location available; Fuji was developed in Japan in the 1930s and released in 1962 as a cross between Red Delicious and Ralls Janet — it has become one of the most popular apple varieties in the world and is now the leading apple in Japan, China, and a major variety in American specialty markets. The large, firm, round fruits with distinctive pink-red and yellow-green streaked skin contain exceptionally crisp, dense, very sweet flesh with a high sugar content (typically 15–18 brix) and mild, pleasant apple flavor that has made Fuji the preferred apple for fresh eating in Asian markets and increasingly in mainstream American grocery stores. The flavor is distinctly sweeter than Gala or Honeycrisp with low acidity. In zone 9 Fuji is a marginal but feasible apple choice in areas that receive adequate winter chilling — inland areas and northern parts of zone 9 typically accumulate more chill hours than coastal areas. Low-chill Fuji selections ('Fuji Babe', 'Low-Chill Fuji') have been developed for warmer climates.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Apple Variety Selection for Texas
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Apple Production in the Gulf South
University of California Cooperative Extension — Fuji Apple Rootstock and Variety Guide
